covered_sentences: Well, Edgar and Isabella heard us laughing, and they cried out, 'Oh, Mamma! Oh, Papa!'
Cathy and I decided to run away, but as we were running, she fell.
They had left the dogs loose, and one of them had Cathy's ankle in his teeth.
She was not crying at all, even though it must have been very painful.
I tried to open the dog's mouth to free Cathy's ankle.
storyboard_assets.prompt:
Composition and camera: horizontal triptych, three panels with white thin straight divider lines, left panel 2 units wide, center panel 1.5 units, right panel 1.5 units. Left panel: camera at ground-level outside the iron gate of Thrushcross Grange courtyard, angled 30 degrees inward toward the estate; iron gate railing edge at left foreground; two figures sprint away from the house in mid-distance, the taller dark-skinned boy slightly ahead of the girl. Center panel: low camera at gravel path level looking across at the fallen girl, a large dog gripping her ankle in the foreground lower-right; the girl lies propped on one elbow, face set firm; the boy crouches beside her reaching toward the dog. Right panel: close shot of the boy's hands prying open the dog's jaws from either side, the girl's booted or bare ankle visible below his hands, gravel ground texture at bottom edge as scale reference. Per-panel character budget: left panel 2 identifiable figures, center panel 2 identifiable figures, right panel 0 identifiable figures (hands only).

Actions and relationships: Left panel: the dark-skinned boy with messy black hair runs in front, dark coat and leather boots; the girl in a dark cotton dress runs just behind him, both bodies leaning forward at speed, arms pumping; no speech bubbles, pure motion. Center panel: the girl has fallen onto the gravel, her posture propped on one elbow, expression controlled and dry-eyed despite obvious pain, jaw tight; a pale dog bites her ankle at lower frame; whisper bubble from the girl's mouth: inside shows a simple running-figure silhouette with an arrow pointing away; the boy crouches close, hands reaching urgently toward the dog, face taut with determination. Right panel: the boy's rough hands grip the dog's jaw bones from either side, fingers splayed to pry it open; no speech bubble; gravel texture visible at lower edge as environmental anchor.

Scene: Thrushcross Grange exterior courtyard and iron gate area, night; flat gravel path surface, stone boundary wall, ornamental iron gate with tall stone pillars capped by stone balls; faint warm amber lamp glow from house windows behind, illuminating the gate metalwork; the stone pillars are clearly taller than both characters; beyond the gate, a straight gravel driveway extends toward trimmed hedges.

Light and color: deep blue-black night sky; only faint orange-amber spill from the house windows behind the gate in the left panel; center and right panels lit by ambient glow catching the characters' faces; cool dark shadows dominate; urgent, tense atmosphere through the cold palette contrasted with the small warm glow.

Subjects and characters: The dark-skinned boy: deep brown skin, short slightly messy black hair, sharp dark eyes, dark gentleman's coat over white shirt visible at collar, dark trousers, leather boots; standing height when upright exceeds the iron gate's top horizontal bar by about half a head. The girl: bright dark eyes, black or dark-brown mid-length slightly disheveled hair, slim build, plain dark cotton dress hem at knee; when standing her head reaches approximately the boy's shoulder level; in center panel she lies on gravel, her upper body from ground to head is roughly equal to the height of the gate's lower horizontal bar from the ground. In the right panel only hands and ankle visible; the gravel surface serves as scale reference.

covered_sentences: Just then the servants ran up.
They took us to the house.
Mr. and Mrs. Linton were horrified at the sight of us.
They said I looked like a gypsy, and that no doubt we were thieves.
'Why, it is Miss Earnshaw,' he said.

covered_sentences: 'Why would Miss Earnshaw be running around the moor barefoot with a gypsy?'
But finally she believed it, and then she felt sorry that her dog had made Cathy's ankle bleed, and she called a doctor and said Cathy must stay as her guest.
But they had not the good manners to invite me to stay with her."
